item.title: Predicting Finish Placements in PUBG
item.description:
Battle royale games have surged in popularity in recent years. The premise of such games is as follows: players are dropped onto a fictional island and fight to be the last person standing. As they roam around the island, they loot for weapons and items crucial for their survival. Players can choose to join a game as a solo player or with a group of friends (4 players maximum). When playing solo, players are immediately eliminated when they are killed. Players not only have to worry about getting killed by other players, but they also have to stay within the shrinking “safe zone,” which effectively forces players into contact with each other. Outside of the “safe zone,” players take damage to their health at increasing rates.

The main goal of this project is to predict a solo player’s finish placement based on their in-game actions. Specifically, we wanted to answer:

  1. How well can we predict who will win the game?
  2. What player actions or features are most predictive of their finish placement?

item.title: Developing Cross-Sectional Methods to Estimate HIV Incidence
item.description: HIV incidence can be determined by following cohorts of HIV-uninfected individuals and quantifying the number of new HIV cases or by using cross-sectional methods. Current cross-sectional methods of estimating HIV incidence utilize multi-assay algorithms (MAAs) to classify individuals as recently or non-recently infected. However, serologic assays that measure general antibody responses to HIV infection may be impacted by viral suppresion (innate or via antiretroviral therapy treatment (ART)), loss of CD4 T-cells, and other factors, resulting in inaccurate incidence estimates. Consequently, we are interested in developing more accurate methods for cross-sectional incidence estimation.

item.title: Spatial Cell Modeling
item.description:
Due to the complexity of cells, it is useful to use computational tools to understand and predict mechanisms of biological processes. Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs) have proven to be successful at modeling various large systems. However, to understand certain biological processes such as bacterial cell division, high spatial resolution is necessary to discern the underlying mechanisms and interactions. ODEs and PDEs, along with the Gillespie algorithm for stochastic modeling, do not provide spatial resolution at a single-particle level; they are all concentration-based methods. MCell, the Free Propagator Reweighting Algorithm (FPR), and Smoldyn are algorithms that provide single-particle resolution, but may be computationally expensive and may not necessarily yield accurate protein dynamics.

I worked with Dr. Margaret Johnson to analyze the strengths and limitations of current state-of-the-art approaches for modeling spatial and temporal dynamics in the cell. I helped establish and perform benchmark tests for comparing the accuracy of a range of methodologies, including single-particle models like those developed in our lab, as well as differential equation based methods, and stochastic simulation methods. We evaluated the effects of molecular geometry, diffusion, and stochasticity on protein dynamics in models of gene expression and cell division.
